flashcards.back: An abstract data type where each element has an associated priority value. Elements are dequeued based on priority rather than insertion order — the element with highest (or lowest) priority is served first, regardless of when it was inserted.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between an ascending (min) priority queue and a descending (max) priority queue?
flashcards.back: In an ascending (min) priority queue, the element with the SMALLEST priority value is dequeued first — used in Dijkstra's and Huffman coding. In a descending (max) priority queue, the element with the LARGEST priority value is dequeued first — used in CPU scheduling and emergency systems.

flashcards.front: What are the core operations of the Priority Queue ADT?
flashcards.back: insert(element, priority) — adds element with priority; deleteMin()/deleteMax() — removes and returns highest-priority element; peek()/findMin()/findMax() — returns highest-priority element without removing; isEmpty() — checks if queue is empty; size() — returns number of elements; changePriority() — updates priority of an existing element.

flashcards.front: When should you use an unsorted implementation vs a sorted implementation for a priority queue?
flashcards.back: Use unsorted array or unsorted linked list when there are MORE insertions than deletions (O(1) insert, O(n) delete). Use sorted array or sorted linked list when there are MORE deletions than insertions (O(n) insert, O(1) delete). For balanced operations, use binary heap (O(log n) for both).

flashcards.front: What is the sorted array trick for implementing a min-priority queue, and why does it work?
flashcards.back: Store elements in DESCENDING order of priority value (smallest priority at the end/index size-1). This makes deleteMin O(1) — simply remove from the end without any searching or shifting. Insert becomes O(n) as elements must be shifted to maintain descending order.

flashcards.front: What are the time complexities for insert, delete, and peek operations in a sorted linked list priority queue?
flashcards.back: Insert: O(n) — must traverse to find correct position. DeleteMin: O(1) — remove from head (smallest priority). Peek: O(1) — examine head. For unsorted linked list: Insert O(1), DeleteMin O(n), Peek O(n).

flashcards.front: Name the applications of priority queues in Dijkstra's algorithm, Huffman coding, and CPU scheduling.
flashcards.back: Dijkstra's shortest path: uses min-priority queue to extract vertex with minimum distance. Huffman coding: uses min-priority queue to repeatedly combine two nodes with smallest frequencies. CPU scheduling: uses max-priority queue to run highest-priority process first.

flashcards.front: Why is a binary heap considered the optimal general-purpose priority queue implementation?
flashcards.back: Binary heap provides O(log n) for both insert AND delete operations, O(1) for peek, and O(n) for building from n elements. This balances the trade-offs between unsorted (fast insert, slow delete) and sorted (slow insert, fast delete) implementations, making it ideal for general use.
